title: Australian GP Reactions: Belacik's Mixed Verdict, Vandesteene's Stunning Upset
meta: Published: 9 March 2026 | By Car Fok, Conventional Race Chronicler & Ex‑Ferrari Driver | F1CC News
caption: Wesley Vandesteene — the surprise hero of Melbourne.
body:
The 2026 Formula 1 Constructors' Championship table shows Mercedes leading Ferrari by three points — 28 to 25 — after the opening race in Melbourne, a gap built not on race-winning dominance but on the quiet efficiency of a P2 and a P5 across both silver cars while the cavallino rampante took victory with one car and a P12 with the other. Twenty‑four hours on from Eetu Väisänen's lights‑to‑flag win, the paddock remains in a divided state of conversation. Väisänen's victory was clinical and commanding, but the events around him exposed vulnerabilities and created opportunities that will shape the championship narrative for weeks.

Ferrari Team Principal Randy Belacik addressed Afonso Cachinho's weekend directly and without softening the language. He told reporters that a P17 qualifying and a P12 finish were not acceptable for a Ferrari driver, then paused before adding that this was, for Cachinho, essentially Friday — one race in difficult conditions against the best on the grid. Belacik made clear that the team knows what Cachinho can do, that they have seen his pace and his racecraft, and that Shanghai will tell a different story. The balance between criticism and conviction was notable. Belacik was not describing a driver whose seat is under immediate threat but one being tested, and perhaps one the team still believes in. Cachinho was handed the seat previously occupied by Liam Dela Cruz — a driver who finished second in the 2025 championship with 226 points before stepping away — and that weight is not something anyone carries lightly. Belacik added that the team did not bring Cachinho in to fail, and that Melbourne was a referendum on whether he belongs, while Shanghai would be the real answer. The words were measured, but the pressure is not.

Meanwhile, the Mercedes garage had quite a different story to tell. Wesley Vandesteene's P2 from a P5 start was the most unexpected result of the day, and the tactical call that delivered it — committing aggressively at the safety‑car restart and emerging in second position — is already being discussed as one of the most decisive single‑lap calls the team has made in recent seasons. Vandesteene, driving only his second full‑season race weekend, kept his composure through the restart and the entire second half, holding his nerve under pressure from a charging field to bring home a result that Mercedes could barely have predicted before the race. He told reporters that he trusted what the team gave him and that they put the car in a position to win, and that in those conditions a driver simply drives what is in front of him. He added that he is happy with the result but knows there is much more to come from him this season. The outcome meant that Mercedes left Australia leading the constructors' championship by that three‑point margin over Ferrari, a reward for placing two cars consistently in the points while their main rival took a dominant win and a zero.

As the paddock digested the results, Michiii MaraDöner's podium from P8 on the grid continued to generate quiet but sustained approval. The Greek driver, one of two on the 2026 grid and still being spoken of in pre‑season as a raw talent finding his feet, produced a fully mature drive to P3 that stilled most of the sceptical conversations overnight. MaraDöner managed the wet chaos after Julian Kondra's crash, held his composure when the safety car reshuffled the order, and was clinical in a race that punished drivers who were anything less. Alpine have not celebrated the result with public fanfare, cautious about loading a rookie with excessive expectation, but behind closed doors the engineering group is reportedly delighted. The car, already capable without being outstanding, now has a driver who can extract a podium from adversity on an early race weekend.

Eetu Väisänen was asked whether winning the opening race in such commanding fashion placed extra pressure on him to repeat the result in Shanghai. His answer was brief: they won, the car was quick, they would go again. For a driver who controlled nearly every lap of a treacherous, chaotic grand prix, he has earned the right to be economical with words. Behind that composed exterior, his engineers will already be studying China's very different compound and surface requirements.

Way too behind was the battle for the remaining points, but Sarah Springfield's P6 for Aston Martin deserved more attention than it received. Starting P18, she worked through the wet confusion methodically and brought home six points that will look meaningful if the car proves to be as competitive as it appeared in Melbourne's specific conditions. Quarter Kevu, finishing P7 with the fastest lap, left Melbourne knowing he was undone by the puncture at the worst possible moment but also knowing that five points from a chaotic, wet season opener still gives him a launchpad for Shanghai. The championship's first race rarely defines it. What comes next will.
